origin: A passage from the New Testament written in Koine Greek, traditionally identified as Luke 22:44, describing Jesus’ agony in Gethsemane and the intensity of his prayer (his sweat “like drops of blood” falling to the ground).
author: Most likely Luke the Evangelist (traditional attribution), writing as part of a Christian Gospel account; alternatively, the wording could be encountered through later manuscript transmission/translation tradition, but the content aligns strongly with Luke.
thoughts: The author/evangelist appears motivated to underscore the seriousness and extremity of the moment—highlighting both profound spiritual anguish and physical suffering—while portraying Jesus’ prayer as intensely earnest. The vivid imagery (“like drops of blood”) likely serves to make the reader feel the gravity of the agony rather than present a clinical detail.
audience: Greek-speaking early Christian communities (or readers/hearers within the Hellenistic Roman world) who were familiar with Gospel narratives and interpreted Jesus’ Passion through Scripture, catechesis, and communal worship.
written_when: Late 1st century AD, commonly dated roughly around 70–90 CE (with many scholars placing Luke after Mark and before/during the broader rise of Gentile Christian communities).
